1: "Help, LORD, for no one is faithful anymore; those who are loyal have vanished from the human race" — the condition of universal unfaithfulness that makes the incarnation necessary. Romans 3:10-12 (drawing on Ps 14) reaches the same conclusion: "There is no one righteous, not even one... there is no one who does good." The psalmist's lament over the disappearance of faithful people sets the stage for the one Faithful Person whose faithfulness covers the unfaithfulness of all: Christ, "the faithful and true witness" (Revelation 3:14), "faithful to the one who appointed him" (Hebrews 3:2).
2: "Everyone lies to their neighbor; they flatter with their lips but harbor deception in their hearts" — Christ as the truth who exposed and overcame universal deception. John 1:14: "the Word became flesh and made his dwelling among us... full of grace and truth." John 8:32: "the truth will set you free." Christ confronted the deception of his generation most directly in Matt 23 (the woes against hypocrites whose lips honored God but whose hearts were far from him — quoting Isaiah 29:13). He is the antithesis of this verse: no flattery, only truth.
3: "May the LORD silence all flattering lips and every boastful tongue" — the prayer for the end of self-serving speech. The New Creation will be a place where all false speech is ended: "Nothing impure will ever enter it, nor will anyone who does what is shameful or deceitful, but only those whose names are written in the Lamb's book of life" (Revelation 21:27). Christ's judgment includes the judgment of words (Matthew 12:36: "everyone will have to give account on the day of judgment for every empty word they have spoken").
4: "Those who say, 'By our tongues we will prevail; our own lips will defend us — who is lord over us?'" — the arrogance of autonomous speech that refuses accountability. Christ answered this claim simply: "there is a judge for the one who rejects me and does not accept my words; the very words I have spoken will condemn them at the last day" (John 12:48). The one who says "who is lord over us?" will discover the answer at the judgment seat of Christ (2 Corinthians 5:10).
5: "'Because the poor are plundered and the needy groan, I will now arise,' says the LORD" — the divine arising in response to the cry of the poor. The incarnation is God's supreme "arising" in response to human groaning — the condescension of the eternal Son into human poverty and suffering (2 Corinthians 8:9: "though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor"). Christ is both the one who arises to defend and the one who became poor in order to make the poor rich.
6: "And the words of the LORD are flawless, like silver purified in a crucible, like gold refined seven times" — Christ as the living Word perfectly purified. John 1:1: "In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God." Unlike human speech (Psalms 12:2-4) which is full of flattery and deception, the divine Word is without any admixture of falsehood. The seven-times-refined purity points to perfection — and Christ's words were confirmed perfect by the testing of Satan's temptation (Matthew 4:1-11; Hebrews 4:15), the cross, and the resurrection.
7: "You, LORD, will keep the needy safe and will protect us forever from this generation" — Christ's promise to his own: "I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand" (John 10:28-29). The protection is absolute and permanent — "forever from this generation" anticipates the eschatological security of those who are in Christ (Romans 8:35-39: nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus).
8: "The wicked freely strut about when what is vile is honored among men" — the cultural inversion that exalts what God calls evil. Christ addressed this specifically: "What people value highly is detestable in God's sight" (Luke 16:15). The church is called not to conform to the pattern of this world (Romans 12:2) — the strutting of the wicked does not set the standard. Christ, who was despised and rejected (Isaiah 53:3), defines honor by the Father's perspective, not the crowd's.
